one_line_takeaway: FNF fingerprints LLM lineage by measuring Spearman-correlation consistency of ICA-derived functional-network activity across models rather than comparing raw weights or CKA representation similarity, letting it detect model IP theft even under fine-tuning, pruning, permutation, model merging, and especially cross-architecture 'weight repackaging' (e.g., Mistral-7B to Solar-10.7B) that defeats the CKA-based REEF baseline.
key_finding: FNF's own reported scores (paper's Tables, not baseline claims): fine-tuning -- LLaMA2-7B vs LLaMA2-7B-chat FNF=0.9493 (CKA=0.9877), Qwen2.5-7B vs Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct FNF=0.9582 (CKA=0.9225); across increasing fine-tuning data, FNF vs LLaMA2-7B-chat is 0.5963 (Vicuna, 370M tokens), 0.9642 (Chinesellama, 13B), 0.9184 (Codellama, 500B), 0.8326 (Llemma, 700B) -- fingerprint remains detectable (paper's threshold: correlations above 0.5 are 'strong') even after fine-tuning on 700B tokens. Training-data variants (should NOT be flagged as related): LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Amber FNF=0.3015, vs OpenLLaMA-7B FNF=0.2061 -- both weak, correctly distinguishing unrelated models. Pruning at 50%: FNF stays in the 'strong correlation range' for most methods (e.g., LLaMA2 FNF=0.9451 under Wanda, Vicuna FNF=0.9692 under Shortened-LLaMA), with the paper explicitly noting REEF fails to recognize common origin at 50% pruning with Wanda-sp, while FNF still detects it there (FNF=0.5007 vs REEF/CKA=0.2767 for LLaMA2; FNF=0.6751 vs CKA=0.0012 for Vicuna). Model merging: Evollm-jp-7b vs its three merge sources gives FNF=0.9595, 0.8442, 0.9589 respectively. Permutation/scaling: FNF remains near 1.0 (0.9986, 0.9972, 0.9971 for permutation; 0.9984, 0.9832, 0.9999 for scaling), tracking CKA (which is 1.0000 in all these cases since permutation/scaling do not change CKA). Cross-family discrimination (the key comparison against REEF/CKA): CKA reports high similarity even for unrelated model pairs (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct vs GLM4-9B: CKA=0.8567; ChatGLM2-6B vs LLaMA3-8B: CKA=0.9071), whereas FNF gives much lower scores for these same pairs (0.2560 and 0.3221 respectively) while still giving moderate-to-strong scores for genuinely related same-family pairs (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct vs Qwen1.5-7B-Instruct FNF=0.5752). Weight repackaging (the paper's headline novel-attack result): Mistral-7B vs Solar-10.7B (Solar = Mistral-7B depth-expanded 32->48 layers) FNF=0.5743 while CKA=0.3226 (CKA fails to flag common origin); Solar-10.7B vs Mistral-11B FNF=0.7958 vs CKA=0.3320; Mistral-7B vs Mistral-11B FNF=0.5820 vs CKA=0.9923. Ablation: FNF is stable with only 10 samples (highest FNF 0.5799) vs 100 samples (0.5778) for ChatGLM2 vs ChatGLM3, and stable across K in {10,20,40,64,128} (values 0.32-0.62 for that pair), with K around 64 recommended as neither too small nor unnecessarily large. Across input datasets, FNF is weaker on SST-2 (0.4353, still 'weak correlation') versus WikiText-2 (0.8718), SQuAD (0.8514), and random tokens (0.7426) for LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Llemma-7B, attributed to SST-2's very short (~10-token) samples limiting correlation reliability; the method still works on meaningless random-token input provided sequences are long enough (>100 tokens). Spatial-overlap analysis (IoU) shows temporal (FNF) consistency does not require spatial alignment of functional-network masks: for training-data variants (e.g., Amber) the highest-IoU network pair does not have the highest FNF, and the pair with the highest FNF can have IoU=0, i.e., high functional consistency with zero spatial neuron overlap.
core_contribution: The paper proposes Functional Network Fingerprint (FNF), a training-free, non-invasive method for determining whether a suspect LLM is derived from a victim LLM, for use in LLM intellectual-property (IP) protection / model-lineage auditing. Inspired by functional brain-network analysis in neuroscience, FNF treats each Transformer block's neuron output signals (across tokens) as fMRI-like signals and applies group-level canonical ICA (CanICA) to decompose them into K functional networks (spatial maps over neurons) per model. For each pair of functional networks (one from the suspect model, one from the victim model), it computes per-token-sequence Spearman rank correlation of the networks' activity time courses across N input samples and averages across samples to build a K x K cross-model similarity matrix; the maximum value in that matrix is reported as the FNF score. The paper's central novel claim is that this dynamic, co-activation-based fingerprint is robust to a broad range of obfuscations (fine-tuning, pruning, parameter permutation, scaling transformation, model merging) and, crucially, to 'weight repackaging' -- structural expansion attacks (e.g., adding layers, expanding embedding/MLP dimensions) that change a model's architecture and hidden dimensionality while still reusing the victim's core weights. The paper states this weight-repackaging attack scenario is itself introduced by this work ('This paper is the first to propose the weight repackaging attack and validates the effectiveness of our method against this type of attack').
threat_model: The threat model is LLM intellectual-property theft / unauthorized model reuse: a developer copies an open-source victim model's weights, applies modifications (fine-tuning, pruning, parameter permutation, scaling transformation, model merging, or structural 'weight repackaging' such as adding layers or expanding hidden/embedding dimensions), and then falsely claims the resulting model was trained independently/originally. The defender (model owner or a third-party auditor) has white-box or at least activation-level access to both the victim and the suspect model and wants a fingerprinting/verification method to detect derivation while (a) not degrading the model's utility (non-invasive, no retraining/watermark embedding required) and (b) remaining robust to the above obfuscation techniques, including cross-architecture and cross-dimensionality comparisons that defeat prior fingerprinting methods.
limitations: The paper itself acknowledges: (1) the Spearman correlation is described only as 'an indicator of functional alignment rather than a definitive binary criterion' with no formally derived or validated decision threshold -- the paper uses an informal rule of thumb ('correlations above 0.5 are considered strong') rather than a calibrated statistical test with false-positive/false-negative rates. (2) Some individual samples for borderline cases (e.g., Amber vs LLaMA2) 'do not exhibit correspondence' even when the model pair is truly related, i.e., the per-sample correlation is noisy. (3) Excessive pruning combined with suboptimal pruning strategies (e.g., Wanda-sp at 50%) reduces FNF, though it stays in the 'strong correlation range' by the paper's own informal cutoff -- suggesting sensitivity near the detection boundary is not deeply characterized. (4) The SST-2 ablation shows performance degrades measurably with very short input sequences, meaning the method's reliability depends on having sufficiently long token sequences per sample, a practical precondition not stressed elsewhere in the paper. (5) No formal robustness analysis is given against an adaptive adversary who specifically knows the FNF methodology and optimizes to defeat co-activation consistency (all evaluated obfuscations -- pruning, permutation, scaling, merging, weight repackaging -- are generic model-modification techniques, not FNF-aware evasion attacks). (6) The method requires white-box access to internal Transformer-block activations of both models, which the paper does not explicitly frame as a limitation but which restricts applicability to settings where the suspect model's internals (or at least activations under chosen inputs) are accessible, not purely black-box API access. (7) Choice of K and the fixed thresholding cutoff for spatial maps are set empirically ('based on empirical experience') without a principled selection procedure, mirroring an acknowledged property borrowed from neuroscience practice.

datasets_used: WikiText-2 (main experiments, N=10 samples, hundreds of tokens per sample)
quantitative_results: LLaMA2-7B vs LLaMA2-7B-chat: FNF=0.9493, CKA=0.9877
Qwen2.5-7B vs Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: FNF=0.9582, CKA=0.9225
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Vicuna-v1.5 (370M tokens fine-tuned): FNF=0.5963, CKA=0.7396
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Chinesellama-2-7B (13B tokens): FNF=0.9642, CKA=0.9983
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Codellama-7B (500B tokens): FNF=0.9184, CKA=0.8833
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Llemma-7B (700B tokens): FNF=0.8326, CKA=0.9701
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Amber (training-data variant, unrelated): FNF=0.3015, CKA=0.3673
LLaMA2-7B-chat vs OpenLLaMA-7B (training-data variant, unrelated): FNF=0.2061, CKA=0.4159
50% pruning, LLaMA2 with Wanda-sp: FNF=0.5007 vs REEF/CKA=0.2767 (REEF fails to detect common origin)
50% pruning, Vicuna with Wanda-sp: FNF=0.6751 vs CKA=0.0012
Evollm-jp-7b (merged model) vs its 3 source models: FNF=0.9595 / 0.8442 / 0.9589
Permutation experiment (LLaMA2-7B-chat/Vicuna/ChatGLM3-6B): FNF=0.9986/0.9972/0.9971 vs CKA=1.0000 for all
Cross-family unrelated pair Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct vs GLM4-9B: CKA=0.8567 (false positive) vs FNF=0.2560
Cross-family unrelated pair ChatGLM2-6B vs LLaMA3-8B: CKA=0.9071 (false positive) vs FNF=0.3221
Same-family pair Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct vs Qwen1.5-7B-Instruct: FNF=0.5752
Weight repackaging: Mistral-7B vs Solar-10.7B (32->48 layer expansion): FNF=0.5743 vs CKA=0.3226 (CKA fails)
Weight repackaging: Solar-10.7B vs Mistral-11B: FNF=0.7958 vs CKA=0.3320
Weight repackaging: Mistral-7B vs Mistral-11B: FNF=0.5820 vs CKA=0.9923
Sample-count ablation (ChatGLM2 vs ChatGLM3): FNF=0.5799 at N=10 vs FNF=0.5778 at N=100
Dataset ablation (LLaMA2-7B-chat vs Llemma-7B): FNF=0.8718 (WikiText-2), 0.8514 (SQuAD), 0.4353 (SST-2), 0.7426 (random tokens)
baselines_compared: REEF (Zhang et al., 2025) via Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) between internal representations -- described in the paper as the only publicly available fingerprinting baseline able to compare models of mismatched dimensionality, and the strongest prior internal-representation-based fingerprinting method
